Q1: I tried to keep a fairly consistent routine, usually studying in the evenings after I'd finished with other responsibilities. I'd dedicate about four hours most days, listening to lecture recordings while taking notes. Being an auditory learner, I found that hearing the material multiple times really helped it stick. I'd organize my week around the nine online courses I was taking, which honestly felt overwhelming at times, but I managed by blocking out specific times for each subject.
Q2: My pattern was somewhat regular, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shake things up. I tried to maintain my evening study blocks, but when assignments piled up, everything shifted into crisis mode. The anxiety would spike and suddenly my careful schedule would fall apart as I scrambled to prioritize what was due soonest. I preferred routine, but the reality of managing so many courses meant my schedule was always a bit unpredictable.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new announcements or seeing if grades had been posted—that always made my heart race a bit. I'd also log in when I needed to access lecture recordings or discussion forums. Sometimes I'd just log in out of habit, almost compulsively checking to make sure I hadn't missed something important. The fear of missing a deadline or update kept me logging in pretty regularly, even when I didn't have specific tasks to complete.
Q4: Yes, eventually logging in became almost automatic, like checking my phone first thing in the morning. It happened gradually—probably around week three or four when I realized how much I needed to stay on top of things. The mindfulness exercises I'd been doing helped me create a calmer morning routine where checking the LMS was just part of waking up. It became less anxiety-inducing once it was just habit rather than something I had to consciously decide to do.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I'd play them at different speeds, sometimes slowing down for complex topics or speeding up for review. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful too—reading how other students understood concepts often clarified things I'd missed. I also appreciated when instructors posted audio summaries or podcasts; those felt like they were made for someone like me.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary reading materials, especially PDFs and long text documents. Reading dense academic text just doesn't stick for me the way hearing information does. I'd sometimes download them with good intentions but never actually get through them. The interactive quizzes I also tended to delay because they triggered my anxiety—I'd worry about getting answers wrong even when they were just for practice.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to assignments or exams. If I could listen to it or if it involved discussion with peers, even better. I needed to feel like the effort would translate into something tangible, whether that was understanding a concept better or improving my grade. Activities that felt disconnected from the course goals just added to my stress without any payoff.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps, honestly. Part of me knew they could help, but with my anxiety, adding anything extra to my plate felt dangerous. Required activities had clear consequences, so I prioritized those even when I was exhausted. Optional ones always got pushed aside because I couldn't justify the mental energy when I was already struggling to keep up with what was mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost all the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone during commutes or while doing chores. I also recorded myself summarizing key concepts and played those back repeatedly. My notes were extensive—I'd rewrite them while listening to recordings, which helped cement the information. A lot of my actual learning happened with headphones in, completely disconnected from the internet.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my own audio notes or listening to downloaded lectures without any LMS activity showing. I'd spend hours walking around my apartment, talking through concepts out loud to myself. Sometimes I'd call a classmate and we'd discuss material over the phone for an hour, but none of that would register anywhere. My best studying often happened when I was technically offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it maybe five or six times over the following week. The LMS would only show that single download, but that recording might have been my primary study material for days. I preferred having everything saved locally anyway because it reduced my anxiety about internet issues or the platform being down when I needed to study.
Q12: Around midterms, everything became harder. My anxiety spiked significantly, and even though my stress was generally manageable, the combination of multiple exams across nine courses was paralyzing. I'd sit down to study and feel this overwhelming sense of dread. The mindfulness intervention helped a little, but there were days when I just couldn't bring myself to engage meaningfully with anything.
Q13: The workload from nine courses was genuinely difficult to manage. When I felt confused about material, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS entirely because logging in reminded me of everything I didn't understand. Fatigue was a constant companion—27 hours of studying per week plus life responsibilities left me drained. Some weeks I'd just go through the motions, clicking through content without really absorbing anything.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something. My body was present but my brain had checked out. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I didn't always have the energy to engage more deeply.
Q15: The practice exams were the worst. I knew they'd help me prepare, but my anxiety about seeing how much I didn't know made me avoid them until the last possible moment. I'd tell myself I'd do them 'tomorrow' for weeks. Even important assignments got postponed when my anxiety was high—I'd convince myself I needed to be in the right headspace to do them justice, which sometimes never came.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my savior. They created intense anxiety spikes, but they also forced me to actually complete things. Without deadlines, I probably would have procrastinated everything indefinitely. I'd often have bursts of LMS activity right before due dates, cramming in video watching and forum participation that I'd been putting off. It wasn't healthy, but it worked.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more, but critical feedback—even constructive criticism—would sometimes make me avoid that course for a few days. I needed time to process it without spiraling. When I did well on something, I'd immediately want to do more work in that course. When I struggled, I'd retreat and focus on other subjects instead.
Q18: The structure helped me stay on track when I couldn't motivate myself internally. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework to follow even when I felt lost. But the structure also felt rigid sometimes—I wished there was more flexibility for students like me who needed to process things at our own pace. The weekly rhythm was predictable, which my anxious brain appreciated.
Q19: Having audio options for content made engagement much easier for me. When courses relied heavily on text-based materials, I struggled more. The discussion forums were well-designed and felt manageable. What made things harder was when multiple assignments clustered together—the course design didn't always account for students taking many courses simultaneously. Clearer audio summaries would have helped enormously.
Q20: I think those students might be like me on my bad days—logging in, clicking around, watching videos, but not actually processing anything because they're overwhelmed or anxious. They might be searching for something that makes sense or trying to find motivation that isn't coming. High activity doesn't mean high comprehension; sometimes it means high confusion or high anxiety.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam scores weren't amazing, but I passed, and a lot of my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials and self-made audio notes. Someone looking at my LMS activity wouldn't see the hours I spent with headphones in, reviewing content. Efficiency matters—some students just don't need to be constantly logged in to learn effectively.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click, how long pages are open. But they can't show whether you're actually paying attention or just have a tab open while you're mentally elsewhere. They definitely miss offline studying, phone calls with classmates, and the quality of engagement versus just the quantity. They measure presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious about a topic and want to learn more, not just pass. For me, engagement feels like being absorbed in a lecture recording, losing track of time because I'm interested. It's mental and emotional investment, not just clicking through content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I carried throughout this course, or how the mindfulness exercises helped me cope even when my mood stayed low. It wouldn't capture the hours of audio review I did offline, or the fact that I often felt surprised by how difficult things were despite putting in real effort. My internal experience—the worry, the struggle, the small victories—none of that shows up in any log.
